texts.rg_comment_classroom: In this photo, I took a picture with my students after the lesson. It was very interactive, and they were really engaged, which shows the class was comfortable and effective.
texts.rg_comment_students:
In this part, I showed them the difference between formal English and real-life English.

I told them that “Forget it” is clear and common, and yeah, people use it a lot. It’s simple and direct.

Then we moved to real-life English. I showed them “Let it slide”. This is what people really say in daily situations.

I told them we’re not gonna replace “Forget it”, it’s still correct and useful.

But the main goal was this… when they hear “Let it slide” in a movie or real life, they don’t get confused. They get it fast and feel it’s normal.

And finally, I used the same technique as before, I let them imagine real situations and use the phrase by themselves so they can really understand it.
texts.rg_comment_presentation:
In this lesson, first I showed them a real-life scene. Not just for watching, but for listening like real life, not like the textbook. We focused on how people really talk, how words get short, how some letters disappear, and what is the difference between academic speech and real speech.

Then we took a simple example, the word “just”. I showed them how the last “t” is often not really pronounced. It becomes softer, faster, and more natural.

After that, we moved it into real class life. I asked a student in a real situation. He answered me in Arabic first. Then we took his answer and changed it into English using just. I told him: use this sentence, “I just got here.”

Then he said it, and he continued speaking in the same natural way.

So the student is not just memorizing words. He is living the language, step by step, like real life. He is not thinking about grammar rules only, he is thinking how people actually speak.

And this is the real goal of the lesson. Not to make English something in the book, but something in the mouth, in the moment, in real situations.
texts.rg_comment_formal_vs_real:
In this part, I showed them the difference between formal English and real-life English.
I told them that Forget it is clear and common, and yeah, people use it a lot. It's simple and direct.

Then we moved to real-life English. I showed them Let it slide. This is what people really say in daily situations.

I told them: we're not gonna replace Forget it. It's still correct and useful.
But the main goal was this… when they hear Let it slide in a movie or real life, they don't get confused. They understand it fast and feel like it's normal.

So now, they can understand both, the book English and the real English.
